title: The Ice and the Inheritance
blurb: A disgraced heir in the frozen dark. Betrayal buried under ice. The truth is a weapon—one wrong move and it shatters them both. Who do you trust when the whole world is built on lies?
comparable: For fans of *The Secret History* by Donna Tartt, *Ice* by Ulla Berkéwicz, *The Firm* by John Grisham

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The heated vestibule of Ice Lab smelled of sweat and sterilizer. Elias Mercer adjusted the thermals under his jumpsuit, fingers trembling just enough to matter. The gloves were a size too small, the left one pinching across the knuckle where the scar from the ice axe had set crooked.

Lila Cho looked up from her workstation. "You're late."

"Traffic." He tossed the clipboard onto her desk. "Core temp readings are stable. Sample’s ready for transfer."

She didn’t touch the clipboard. Her coffee cup sat untouched beside the keyboard, steam curling in slow arcs. "Ren will take it down. You’re not cleared for the vault today."

Elias stayed by the door. The station’s hum seeped through the floor—generators, ductwork, distant clangs from the mechanical bay. Somewhere beyond Black Ridge Dormitory, the aurora flickered behind the reinforced windows.

Ren Marquez entered, his parka already zipped to the chin. He didn’t look at Elias. "Got the transport sled prepped. Lila, you want the manifest double-checked?"

She tapped a key. A printer in the corner whirred. "Two copies. Hand-carry to Dry Glove. Seal the outer crate before you leave."

Elias stepped forward. "I should be the one—"

Lila’s gaze stayed on the screen. "Commander’s orders. Field rotations suspended for the week."

Ren slung the clipboard under his arm. "Guess that means you’re stuck with the incinerator detail, Mercer."

Elias exhaled. The breath fogged the inside of his goggles. He didn’t wipe them. He kept his hands still.

Lila finally met his eyes. "Unless you’ve got something to add?"

He almost said it—the assay numbers, the pressure anomalies, the proof that Julian’s avalanche report had buried a fault line that shouldn’t have slipped. Instead he nodded. "Just the usual. Make sure the outer crate gets the tamper seal."

Ren grinned, teeth yellow in the fluorescent light. "Trust me, I’ll treat it like my own."

The door hissed shut. Lila stood, walked to the window, and pressed her palm against the cold glass. Outside, the snow cat rumbled toward the vault, headlights cutting pale tunnels through the storm.

Elias waited until the sound faded. Then he reached into his pocket and palmed the flash drive from his thermals. It was warm from his body heat. He tapped the side of the workstation twice—once for each failed test—and slipped the drive into the underside of the desk panel. The magnet held it tight.

A bleep from Lila’s terminal. She swiveled back to the screen, fingers flying across the keyboard. The printer fired again. A single sheet slid out, bearing a file number and a timestamp.

Elias picked it up. His name scrawled in the margin, half erased. Below it, a line of coordinates he didn’t recognize.

He folded the paper into the breast pocket of his jumpsuit, over the hidden pocket where the real assay data lived.

The station intercom crackled. Captain Koval’s voice, clipped and authoritative: "Mandatory safety briefing in twenty. All personnel not on critical shift, report to the mess hall."

Lila didn’t look up. "You’re not on the roster for fieldwork."

Elias touched the pocket. The flash drive pressed against his ribs.

He stepped into the corridor. The incinerator chute waited at the far end, a black maw exhaling warm rot. Somewhere down the hall, a door slammed. Boots echoed on metal grating.

He walked faster.

Chapter 2

The mess hall stank of burnt coffee and synthetic protein. Boots squeaked on the vinyl floor as personnel shuffled to their seats. Elias Mercer stood near the back, thumb tracing the edge of Lila’s printout in his pocket. The paper had softened under his grip.

Captain Renn Koval entered from the side corridor, parka damp at the shoulders. He didn’t remove it. The room quieted without him speaking.

“Listen up,” Koval said. “Ice Lab’s field advances are suspended effective today. Personnel reductions: Dr. Cho’s team will operate at sixty percent capacity. Mr. Mercer—” He paused. Eyes flicked to Elias. “—is reassigned from Ice Lab to waste disposal. Effective immediately.”

A fork clattered against a tray. The sound fractured the silence.

Elias felt the heat rise to his collar. He kept his face blank, teeth pressed together.

Lila didn’t look his way. She stirred her coffee with measured circles.

Koval tapped a screen on the lectern. A file icon appeared on the overhead display. “Mercer Trust corporate code 7-14 flagged Mr. Mercer’s behavior as erratic during sample transfer protocol yesterday. This constitutes a Tier 3 safety risk.”

A murmur rippled through the mess. The woman beside Elias exhaled through her nose, a sharp hiss.

“Tier 3 means temporary demotion pending psychological review,” Koval continued. “Mr. Mercer will report to the incinerator bay at 0800 daily. No exceptions.”

Elias opened his mouth.

Koval lifted a hand. “The decision is final.”

“Sir,” Elias said. “I was following protocol. The sample needed direct transfer to Dry Glove. Ren was already suited—”

“Corporate code supersedes station protocol,” Koval cut in. “The flag came from Julian Mercer’s office in Geneva. We don’t question Tier 3 designations.”

The room shifted. A chair scraped. Someone coughed into a gloved fist.

Lila finally turned. Her eyes held nothing—no pity, no acknowledgment. “Elias, you’re dismissed. Waste has priority.”

He wanted to rip the parka from his shoulders. Instead he nodded, stepped back, and his boot caught a metal leg of a bench. The scrape echoed.

No one moved to help.

The intercom bleeped. Koval’s voice shot through the speakers: “Mercer Trust corporate code 7-14 activated. Station-wide access restriction in effect for Mr. Mercer. No field gear, no lab access, no external comms without prior authorization.”

A digital lock clicked somewhere in the corridor outside. Elias felt the denial like a blade between ribs.

He pushed through the door into the chill vestibule. The incinerator chute yawned at the far end, a rusted throat belching lukewarm air. His hands shook as he unzipped his parka. The cold bit at his throat.

Footsteps. He turned.

Ren Marquez leaned against the wall, arms crossed. “Heard about the downgrade. Classic Mercer luck—first Julian gets buried, now you.”

Elias didn’t answer. He stripped off his gloves and tossed them onto the nearest bench.

Ren pushed off the wall. “You really think you can outrun what you did?”

Elias kept walking. The door to Black Ridge Dormitory hissed open. He stepped inside, boots thudding on the metal grate.

The corridor stretched, lit by flickering fluorescents. His bunk was the third door on the left. Inside, the single bed was made tight enough to bounce a quarter. A photograph of Julian, younger and grinning on a rock face, sat on the shelf above the desk.

Elias tore it in half without hesitation. The paper edges fluttered to the floor. He crumpled the pieces into a ball and hurled it at the waste bin. It missed.

He knelt, scooped up the scraps. As he straightened, his knuckles brushed the underside of the desk. A seam. A catch.

He pressed.

The floor panel popped free. Beneath it, a small metal box wedged into the insulation. Elias pulled it out. It wasn’t his. He knew the combination—Julian’s birthday reversed.

The lid clicked open.

Three drives. Two standard. One black, sleek, with a voiceprint lock.

Elias exhaled. The box clattered onto the bed.

He set the black drive aside and powered up his terminal. The screen lit his face in blue. He typed a string: *the wind howls where the snow lies still*

Three hours. That’s how long it took for the reply to route through delayed satellite, bounce off a comms buoy in the Southern Ocean, and land in his inbox.

“Package secured. Route cleared. ETA Geneva 36 hours. You still planning to run?”

Elias stared at the screen. Outside, the wind howled against the dormitory walls.

He didn’t answer. He shut the terminal down. The black drive fit into his palm, cold as a bullet.

A bleep from the corridor. The station intercom crackled again—Koval’s voice, dry and final: “All personnel remain in quarters until further notice. Ice Lab operations suspended pending Mercer Trust audit.”

Elias slipped the drive into his jumpsuit pocket, over his ribs.

He reached under the bed. His fingers closed around the grip of the ice axe he’d kept stashed since the climb.

The blade glinted in the dim light.

He didn’t wipe it clean.

Chapter 3

The incinerator bay smelled of ammonia and old fat. Steam curled from the chute like gray lace. Elias Mercer fed a crate of melted sample jars into the grinder. The blades whined, grinding the glass to grit.

Behind him, the door groaned open. Boots scraped against the metal grate.

“Mercer.”

He didn’t turn. “Koval.”

The captain’s parka hood brushed the ceiling light fixture. “You’re late.”

“Traffic’s bad this time of day.”

A laugh, sharp and humorless. “You always were a smartass.”

The chute coughed, spat a cloud of white ash into the air. Elias coughed into his sleeve.

Koval stepped closer. The parka’s zipper pulled taut over his chest. “They’re auditing Dry Glove today. Corporate’s sending a rep from Geneva.”

Elias dropped the empty crate onto the pile. “Lila coordinating?”

“Cho? No. Mercer Trust rep. Woman named Dr. Voss. Know her?”

Elias’s stomach tightened. “No.”

Koval watched him for a beat. The incinerator’s glow painted half his face orange. “Funny name for a rep.”

Elias shouldered past him. “I’ve got rounds.”

Koval’s hand shot out, clamped onto Elias’s arm. The grip was loose, almost polite. “Sitrep. You’re restricted to this bay until audit’s done. No lab, no comms, no—”

“I know the code, Captain.” Elias twisted free. “7-14. Erratically dangerous.”

Koval’s fingers flexed at his side. “You’re playing a dangerous game, Mercer.”

Elias walked backward toward the door, boots thudding. “All games are dangerous. Just depends on who’s holding the dice.”

The captain didn’t follow.

The corridor outside the bay stretched like a throat. Fluorescents buzzed. Elias exhaled, slow, through his nose. He needed to get to Black Ridge. The dormitory door hissed open, beckoning.

His bunk was empty, the sheets tucked military-tight. The snapped photograph of Julian lay in the waste bin, edges glued to a smear of spit.

Elias knelt. His fingers probed the underside of the desk. The catch gave way.

The metal box slid out. Cold seeped through the insulation. Elias set it on the bed.

He typed the code.

Three drives. Two he recognized—his own backups, stale from years ago. The third was black, ceramic, with a small microphone icon beside the port.

Voiceprint lock.

Julian’s.

Elias turned the drive over. A chill fingered his ribs.

He snapped the box shut. The catch clicked louder than he meant.

A knock at the door.

Elias froze. “Yes?”

“It’s Lila.”

He slid the box under the bed. “Come in.”

She pushed the door open, parka damp at the shoulder seams. The hood framed her face, dark eyes scanning the room. “I heard about the audit.”

Elias zipped his jumpsuit to the collar. “Busy day for corporate.”

She stepped inside, boots clicking on the grate. “You’re not supposed to be here.”

“I live here.”

“Not in this room. Not while Dry Glove’s being audited.”

Elias crossed his arms. “You running security now?”

Lila unzipped her parka halfway, left it open. “Koval’s paranoid. You’re flagged Tier 3. That means you don’t get privacy.”

A beat. The intercom bleeped in the corridor, a tinny voice: “Station-wide lockdown in effect. Mercer Trust audit. All personnel remain in quarters until notified.”

Lila’s eyes flicked to the waste bin. “You burned the photo.”

Elias didn’t answer.

She exhaled through her nose. “Julian would’ve wanted proof of survival.”

“I’m not burning my brother. I’m burning the lie.”

Lila stepped closer. The parka sleeve brushed his wrist. “You always hated the lie.”

Elias turned to the desk, tapped the terminal. The screen lit. He pulled up a cached weather map—wind speeds, storm tracks. “You always hated the truth.”

A silence thick as ice.

Lila’s gloved hand settled on the terminal edge. “Elias.”

He didn’t look at her.

“You keep digging, you’ll hit something you can’t climb out of.”

Elias minimized the map. A notification blinked in the corner—satellite relay confirmed. Delayed transmission window open.

He swiveled the screen toward her. “You going to report that?”

Lila’s fingers twitched. She didn’t answer.

Elias typed: *the wind howls where the snow lies still*

The reply timer spun. 03:59… 03:58…

Lila’s voice dropped, low and flat. “They’re going to bury you, Elias. Not here. Geneva. The trust doesn’t forgive betrayal.”

The timer spun. 01:12… 01:11…

Elias leaned back. “Then I’ll dig.”

The terminal bleeped. Message received.

Lila’s parka rustled as she turned. “This ends tonight.”

The door hissed shut.

Elias waited five breaths. Then he pulled the black drive from his pocket.

He plugged it in. The terminal prompted for voice recognition.

Elias hesitated. He hadn’t heard Julian’s voice in five years. Not since the ice screamed.

He cleared his throat. Spoke into the microphone.

“Julian Mercer.”

The drive whirred. A status bar crept across the screen. Authentication: voiceprint match. 97%.

Elias’s pulse hammered in his throat.

The drive opened.

A single file: *J_Mercer_Summit_Report_Avalanche_2022.pdf*

He double-clicked.

The pdf loaded, pixelated from corruption. A timestamp glared: 14:23:11 UTC, six days after the accident.

Julian’s signature scrawled in wet ink.

Elias’s breath fogged the screen.

A floorboard creaked in the corridor.

He slammed the lid. The drive popped out. He caught it, stuffed it back into his pocket.

The door handle turned.

Elias swiveled the terminal toward the wall, hit sleep. The screen went dark.

Lila stepped inside. Her hood was down now. Hair pulled into a tight knot. She held a datapad, scrolling.

“Audit’s wrapping up,” she said. “Voss wants a final tour of Dry Glove. You’re coming.”

Elias stood. “No thanks.”

“Protocol. You’re Tier 3. You don’t get to decline.”

Elias brushed past her. The black drive pressed into his ribs, cold as a bullet.

He pulled his parka from the hook. “Lead the way, Doctor.”

Lila’s lips thinned. She didn’t follow.

Chapter 4

The Dry Glove Vault hummed like a sleeping beast. Racks of servers glowed blue in the dark, their fans a steady whisper. Elias adjusted the headset. The Geneva feed flickered on the main screen—a high-definition split of Julian Mercer and three Mercer Trust executives behind a sleek glass desk.

Julian leaned forward, fingers steepled. “Our expanded operations in the Ross Dependency represent not just economic growth, but a commitment to sustainable stewardship of Earth’s last frontier.”

Elias’s thumb hovered over the volume dial. The words were smooth, practiced. A performance.

On-screen, a journalist’s hand shot up. “What about allegations of unauthorized mineral extraction in the Weddell quadrant?”

Julian didn’t flinch. “All activities are fully compliant with UN Environmental Protocol Chapter 7. I’d be happy to forward the audit logs upon request.”

The journalist nodded, scribbling.

Elias reached into his pocket. The black drive nestled against his thigh. He plugged it into the terminal’s hidden port. The system recognized the hardware, asked for the encryption key. He typed slowly, careful not to mis-key.

Behind him, the vault door hissed open. Footsteps—two sets—echoed on the metal grate.

“—still locked down from the audit?” A woman’s voice. Dr. Voss.

Elias didn’t turn. The screen glitched as the hijack initiated.

Julian continued, “We’re not just mining ice or stone. We’re extracting knowledge. Data that will redefine climate science for generations.”

Elias’s fingers danced over the keyboard. The feed stuttered. A Mercer IT icon blinked red in the corner.

“Mercer.” Voss again, closer.

On-screen, Julian’s smile tightened. He reached for his water glass.

Elias exhaled. The hijack protocol engaged. Julian’s voice streamed into the Geneva feed, but the source shifted—now originating from the Dry Glove terminal.

Julian frowned. “—data that will redefine climate science for generations.”

The Mercer executives shifted in their seats. One checked his watch.

Elias typed: *sustainable stewardship?*

Julian’s eyes flicked to the camera. “I apologize—technical difficulty. Let me—”

Elias’s thumb pressed Enter.

The video froze. Julian’s face pixelated. The audio dropped out.

Then it restarted. But Julian wasn’t speaking anymore.

A new image appeared—a timestamped file: *J_Mercer_Transcript_2023_06_14.txt*

Julian’s voice, raw and unfiltered, filled the room.

“—the penguin rookery at Black Ridge is a wash. Too visible. We reroute the barge to Cape Roberts. Under the ice shelf, the sonar won’t catch us.”

The executives leaned in, rapt.

Elias leaned back. The vault’s cold air bit through his parka.

Voss’s boots stopped behind him. “You’re not authorized for corporate feeds.”

Elias didn’t look up. “Corporate feed’s down.”

Julian’s voice continued: “—fuel reserves. Take twenty percent from McMurdo’s winter cache. Koval won’t question it.”

Voss’s hand landed on Elias’s shoulder, fingers sharp as bone. “Turn it off.”

Elias swiveled in the chair. Her face was a mask of controlled irritation. “It’s a glitch. I’m fixing it.”

“You’re broadcasting Mercer Trust internal communications to a live feed.”

Elias tapped the keyboard. The frozen Julian flickered back to life—smooth, corporate again. “Looks fixed.”

Voss’s jaw worked. “Shut it down.”

Elias minimized the window. Opened a terminal prompt. Typed:

> Reroute complete. McMurdo fuel cache 20% allocated to Cape Roberts barge.

The command executed. The vault’s lights dimmed for a half-second—system load.

Voss’s datapad buzzed. She glanced at the screen. Her fingers paled around the edges.

“That’s restricted logistics.”

Elias pulled the black drive, unplugged it. “Glitch.”

Julian’s voice still echoed in the box speakers, a ghost in the wires.

Voss stepped back. “You’re compromised.”

“No.” Elias stood. The chair rolled into the rack. The servers hummed louder, like a held breath. “I’m consistent.”

Voss turned toward the door. Then paused. “Koval’s going to have your badge.”

Elias watched the playback of Julian’s betrayal shrink to a thumbnail on the screen. “Let him.”

The vault door sealed behind her.

Silence settled, thick as snow outside.

Elias packed the drive into his pocket. His breath fogged the screen once, then cleared.

He reached for the terminal’s power switch.

A red light blinked on the rack. A server’s status bar read: *Remote admin session active. Source: Black Ridge Bunk 12.*

Elias’s fingers hovered over the switch.

He knew that bunk number.

He hadn’t been in Black Ridge since before the audit. Since Lila’s warning.

He turned.

The vault’s emergency light cast long shadows. The racks stood like silent sentinels.

Then the terminal beeped.

A message window opened.

Unknown User: You took something that wasn’t yours.

Elias stared at the screen. The cursor blinked. Waiting.

A new line appeared.

Unknown User: Now I’m taking something of yours.

The screen went dark.

The emergency light died.

In the black, the servers began to reboot, their fans roaring to life in staggered pulses—like a heartbeat.

Then the glow returned.

Not from the screen.

From the corridor.

A flashlight beam sliced through the dark, illuminating a single word spray-painted on the vault door:

> **STILL**

Chapter 5

The Command Hub smelled like burnt coffee and static. Julian’s face filled the holographic display above the comms desk, but the image stuttered—satellite drift, maybe, or Koval’s network throttling. Not Julian’s fault. He never lost signal.

“Renn,” Julian said. “I need twenty percent of McMurdo’s winter fuel cache rerouted to the *Edith*.”

Koval’s fingers paused over the keyboard. The command console lit her face in green, then amber. “Twenty percent?”

“Cape Roberts,” Julian said. “Under the shelf. Sonar won’t pick it up.”

Koval leaned back in her chair. The leather creaked. A map of the Ross Ice Shelf glowed behind her, red pinpoints marking fuel depots. McMurdo’s winked.

“Winter reserves are locked,” Koval said. “Emergency protocol.”

Julian’s jaw tightened. “Protocol can be adjusted.”

Koval exhaled through her nose. She reached for her datapad, swiped open a private channel, typed a single line:

> Fuel override denied. Environmental compliance violation. I will not risk station safety for your barge.

The message sent.

Julian didn’t wait. “Renn, I’m not asking.”

Koval’s eyes flicked to the hologram, then back to the pad. She tapped another command. The McMurdo fuel inventory scrolled. Twenty percent. Gone.

“Done,” she said.

Julian’s image blinked out. The hub’s speakers hissed with residual static.

Koval didn’t look at anyone. She closed the inventory tab, opened the station’s environmental audit log, and added a single sentence at the bottom:

> Fuel transfer: 14,200 liters. Destination: Cape Roberts. Reason: Emergency resupply of autonomous research station *Edith*. No environmental impact expected.

She saved. Locked the pad.

Peterson, the comms tech, cleared his throat. “Incoming call from Dr. Cho.”

Koval didn’t answer right away. She stared at the blank screen where Julian’s face had been, then at the fuel transfer confirmation blinking in the corner of her vision.

“Patch it through,” she said.

The comms board chime rang once. Lila’s face snapped into view—cold, clinical, framed by the Ice Lab’s sterile white walls.

“Commander,” Lila said. “We have an issue.”

Koval crossed her arms. “Define issue.”

“Tier 3 security flag on Waste Disposal Bay,” Lila said. “Again.”

Koval’s stomach dropped. Waste Disposal Bay was Mercer’s designated drop point for classified data couriers. It had been for years.

“It’s Voss,” Koval said.

“Confirmed,” Lila said. “He hasn’t left the bay in twenty minutes. He triggered the drone scanner with an unregistered terminal.”

Koval rubbed her temples. The pressure behind her eyes was a familiar storm. “He’s not clearing waste. He’s transmitting.”

Lila’s expression didn’t change. “We need to process that flag.”

“Or?”

“Or Mercer Trust legal will have his badge by morning.”

Koval exhaled. “You countermanded the fuel transfer.”

Lila’s eyes flickered. She knew. Of course she knew.

“Environmental risk,” Lila said. “We can’t justify twenty percent of winter reserves for a private barge. Not with UN compliance pending.”

Koval stared at the ceiling. The hub’s fluorescents buzzed. “You just kneecapped Julian’s extraction route.”

“We follow protocol,” Lila said. “That’s all.”

Koval opened a private file. Julian’s voice, clear as glass, filled the room:

> —take twenty percent from McMurdo’s winter cache. Koval won’t question it.

She minimized the file. Closed her eyes.

“Protocol,” Koval repeated. “Right.”

Lila said nothing. The Ice Lab’s overhead lights reflected off her glasses.

Koval opened the fuel transfer log. The deletion timestamp was hers. The denial reason was Lila’s.

She couldn’t fix it without exposing both of them.

“Commander,” Lila said. “Do I need to escalate?”

Koval opened a blank file. Typed a single line:

> Voss. Waste Disposal Bay. Tier 3 flag active.

She locked the file. Saved it to a server labeled *CORRESPONDENCE_BLACK*.

“No,” Koval said. “I’ll handle it.”

Lila’s image dissolved into static. The comms board chimed again—this time, an encrypted file from Julian. Koval didn’t open it.

She pulled up the station’s fuel budget. McMurdo’s winter reserves were tight. Emergency protocols required seventy-two hours of reserve at all times. Twenty percent wasn’t just cutting it close—it was gambling with lives.

She opened another file. A list of personnel. At the top, Elias Voss.

She typed:

> Authorized entry to Waste Disposal Bay. Tier 3 override.

She sent the order.

The screen refreshed. The fuel transfer log remained altered. Julian’s barge wouldn’t get its fuel.

At least not today.

---

Waste Disposal Bay smelled like ozone and old paper. The incinerator’s glow pulsed through the grate under the sorting conveyor, casting long shadows across the sorting tables. Elias stood between two stacks of crates, his gloved hands steady as he fed a thin cable into a terminal no one had used in years.

The terminal beeped. Green.

> Access granted: High-security clearance. Warm Lab Archives.

Elias exhaled. The cable snaked from the terminal to his datapad. He tapped a sequence—from his research days. A backdoor. One Lila didn’t know about.

The screen flickered. A system alert popped up:

> STATION-WIDE SYSTEMS AUDIT INITIATED. EXTERNAL CONNECTION DETECTED.

Elias cursed under his breath. He hadn’t triggered a full systems check. Someone else had.

He typed faster:

> Audit override. Mercer Trust clearance. Voss, Elias.

The alert vanished.

Then a new one appeared:

> CORPORATE COMPLIANCE CHECK ACTIVE. DRY GLOVE VAULT LOCKDOWN REQUIRED.

Elias’s fingers froze.

Dry Glove Vault.

Julian’s legal team was running a surprise compliance check.

He looked at the cable in his hand. It led to a black box taped under the sorting table. Inside, a single encrypted drive. His drive.

He yanked the cable free. The terminal beeped in protest.

A voice cut through the bay’s hum.

“You’re not supposed to be in here.”

Elias turned.

Koval stood in the doorway, parka zipped to her chin, gloves tucked in her belt. The light from the incinerator haloed her like a judge’s lamp.

Elias stuffed the datapad into his pocket. The cable coiled in his fist.

“Tier 3 flag,” Koval said. “Waste Disposal access. Not authorized.”

Elias didn’t answer.

Koval stepped forward. The bay’s temperature dropped with her presence. “You triggered a systems alert.”

Elias met her gaze. “Didn’t know the station was allergic to audits.”

Koval’s eyes flicked to the crates, the cable, the black box under the table. “You’re transmitting.”

Elias said nothing.

Koval reached for her datapad. Her fingers hovered over the screen. Then she stopped.

A new alert blared from the bay’s speakers:

> CORPORATE COMPLIANCE CHECK FAILED. DRY GLOVE VAULT LOCKDOWN ENGAGED. BIOMETRIC OVERRIDE DETECTED.

Koval’s jaw tightened.

The bay’s lights dimmed. Emergency red pulsed through the grate.

Elias looked at the vault’s remote access terminal on the wall. The screen flashed:

> ACCESS DENIED: MERCER TRUST OVERRIDE ACTIVE.

Koval’s datapad buzzed. She glanced down. A message from Lila:

> What the hell?

Koval’s thumb moved. She tapped:

> Lock the vault.

Then she looked at Elias.

“You just declared war on the trust,” she said.

Elias met her stare.

The bay’s incinerator roared behind him, hungry.

Chapter 6

The Ice Lab’s server room smelled like burnt capacitors and coffee left too long in a chipped mug. Elias crouched behind a rack of black-box servers, his breath clouding in the cold. The cable snaked from his wrist-mounted device into the maintenance port of Station AI node 7—one he’d wired himself during the last thaw cycle, before he’d been locked out of the grid.

A voice cut through the hum of the servers.

“You trying to crash the station or just quit?”

Technician Ren Marquez leaned against the server rack, arms crossed, eyes on the flickering monitor where Elias’s code was running.

Elias didn’t look up. “Node 7’s CPU temp’s spiking. Gotta reset it.”

Ren scoffed. “Temp’s fine. And you’re not cleared for node 7.”

“Since when do clearance rules apply to hardware?”

“Since Koval revoked your clearance.” Ren tapped the monitor. “Station AI’s locked down. You’re not even supposed to be in here.”

Elias’s fingers didn’t pause. “Then why are you?”

Ren grinned. “Because Lila asked me to make sure you didn’t.”

A red warning flashed on the monitor:

> SYSTEMS AUDIT INITIATED. EXTERNAL CONNECTION DETECTED.

Elias cursed under his breath and typed faster. “Ren, move.”

“Nope.” Ren didn’t budge. “You’re about to get flagged again. And this time, it’s Tier 4.”

The monitor beeped. A new alert popped up:

> CORPORATE COMPLIANCE CHECK ACTIVE. DRY GLOVE VAULT LOCKDOWN REQUIRED.

Ren’s grin faded. He leaned in, squinting at the screen. “Vault lockdown? What the hell—”

Elias yanked the cable free. The monitor flickered, then died. Black screen.

Ren stepped back. “You just triggered a compliance check. Julian’s team’s going to shit.”

“Good,” Elias said. He unplugged the power strip from the rack. The servers groaned, then went dark.

Ren’s glove hit Elias’s chest. “The hell are you doing?”

Elias shoved him back. “Making sure Julian’s legal team doesn’t erase what’s mine.”

Ren stumbled, caught himself on a server rack. “You’re not making sense.”

“Ask Lila.” Elias coiled the cable, stuffed it into his pocket. “Ask anyone who remembers my name.”

Ren blocked the door. “Station AI’s going to log this. Koval’s going to know.”

Elias looked at the dead monitor, then at Ren. “Then she’ll know I’m done asking.”

Ren’s radio crackled. Static, then Koval’s voice:

“Ren, report to Command Hub. Now.”

Ren’s eyes flicked to Elias, then back to the radio. He hesitated, then tapped the transmit button. “On my way.”

He didn’t move.

Elias stepped past him, shoulder brushing Ren’s arm. “Go.”

Ren didn’t go. “You’re going to the Dry Glove Vault.”

Elias didn’t answer.

Ren’s jaw tightened. “Lila’s in there. She’s got Julian’s team on the line. They’re waiting for the vault’s audit.”

Elias paused. “Then she’ll get what she wants.”

“You don’t understand.” Ren’s voice dropped. “The vault’s biometric lock’s been overridden. It’s not just a compliance check. It’s a purge.”

Elias turned. The server room’s emergency lights cast long shadows. “Override by who?”

Ren’s radio crackled again. Koval’s voice, sharper: “Ren. Now.”

Ren unclipped the radio from his belt, tossed it onto the floor. It skittered under the servers.

“Julian,” Ren said. “Or someone using his clearance.”

Elias’s pulse thudded in his throat. He stuffed his hands into his pockets. “Then I’ll use mine.”

Ren shook his head. “You don’t have clearance anymore.”

Elias walked to the door. “I never needed it.”

---

The Dry Glove Vault’s antechamber was a tomb of white light and silence. The door’s biometric scanner glowed red, pulsing like a heartbeat. Lila stood in front of it, datapad in hand, her breath steady. Behind her, Julian’s legal liaison—a woman Elias didn’t recognize—watched the scanner with thinly veiled impatience.

Lila didn’t turn around. “You’re late.”

Elias stepped forward. The floor’s anti-static tiles hummed under his boots. “You always did like an audience.”

Lila finally looked at him. Her face was a mask. “You triggered a systems audit. That’s a Tier 4 violation.”

“So’s corporate espionage.” Elias stopped three feet from the scanner. “I’m here to countermand it.”

The legal liaison’s fingers twitched. “Identity verification required.”

Elias pulled a small device from his pocket—a modified biometric scanner he’d taken from the Waste Disposal Bay after Koval left. He tapped it twice, then held it up to the vault’s scanner.

The scanner beeped. Green.

> ACCESS GRANTED: MERCER TRUST CLEARANCE.

The legal liaison’s lips parted. Lila’s fingers tightened around her datapad.

The vault door hissed open.

Lila stepped forward. “Elias—”

He didn’t wait. He walked into the vault, the door sealing shut behind him.

The vault was a cylinder of white walls and humming servers, the air thick with ozone. In the center, the main terminal blinked with a single command:

> CORPORATE COMPLIANCE PURGE INITIATED. 00:04:12 REMAINING.

Elias moved to the terminal. His datapad connected instantly. He typed:

> PURGE ABORT: OVERRIDE MERCER TRUST CLEARANCE.

The terminal flickered. A new message appeared:

> OVERRIDE CONFIRMED.

Then, without warning, the terminal’s screen went black. The vault’s lights dimmed to emergency red.

A voice crackled over the vault’s speakers—Julian’s voice, smooth and calm:

“Elias. You should have stayed in the shadows.”

The vault door’s lock engaged with a metallic *clunk*.

Lila’s voice came through the speakers, sharp and clear: “Elias. Stand down.”

Elias didn’t move. He stared at the terminal. The purge timer was gone. In its place, a single line of text:

> ACCESS DENIED: MERCER TRUST OVERRIDE ACTIVE.

The vault’s lights pulsed once, then died.

In the darkness, Elias heard the sound of a datapad hitting the floor—Lila’s. Then, footsteps retreating.

The door didn’t open.

The vault’s emergency lights flickered back on, casting long shadows.

Elias exhaled. The override was his.

But the purge wasn’t stopped.

It was just beginning.

Chapter 7

The Ice Lab smelled like antifreeze and sweat. A puddle of melted ice tracked from the ceiling to the floor, glinting under the fluorescent lights. Elias knelt beside the ruptured fuel line, his gloves black with grease. The spill hissed against the hot pipe, sizzling into steam.

Lila stood three feet away, arms crossed, watching Ren Marquez tighten a clamp on the ruptured line.

“Funny how that only happens when he’s not around,” Lila said.

Elias didn’t look up. He wiped his hands on his coveralls, leaving streaks of oil. “Corrosion. Happens fast in this humidity.”

Ren didn’t meet his eyes. “You’d know.”

Lila exhaled through her nose. “Cut the bullshit. We both know why you’re here.”

Elias stood, rolling his shoulders. The Ice Lab’s observation deck overlooked the Ross Ice Shelf through reinforced glass. Outside, the wind howled, pelting the station with ice. A storm was coming.

“Station AI flagged the leak,” he said. “I just followed procedure.”

Lila’s glove tapped the datapad strapped to her arm. “Procedure says you don’t touch anything outside Waste Disposal.”

Elias picked up a wrench from the cart. “Waste Disposal’s not a death sentence. But this lab is.”

Ren finally looked at him. “What’s that supposed to mean?”

Elias didn’t answer. He tossed the wrench onto the cart. The metal clanged, echoing off the metal walls.

Lila’s jaw twitched. “You’re not here to fix pipes.”

Elias stepped toward her. “No. I’m here because the Dry Glove Vault’s purge timer just reset. Someone restarted it.”

The datapad slipped from Lila’s fingers. It hit the floor and skittered under a server rack. She didn’t move to retrieve it.

Ren frowned. “Purge timer?”

Lila’s voice was quiet. “It’s a compliance check. It doesn’t reset unless Julian’s team overrides it.”

Elias nodded. “Someone wants the vault cleaned.”

A red light flashed on the lab’s status board.

> EMERGENCY ALERT: STATION-WIDE

The speakers crackled. Koval’s voice cut through the static.

“Attention all personnel. Rendezvous Point Delta failure. Repeat, Rendezvous Point Delta failure. All non-essential personnel evacuate to secondary shelter. Non-compliance will result in restricted privileges.”

Lila’s hand flew to her radio. “Delta’s on the east side. That’s ten minutes from here.”

Ren grabbed his parka from the hook. “What’s at Delta?”

Elias didn’t move. “Fuel depot. The one that feeds the generators.”

Lila’s eyes locked onto him. “You triggered it.”

The speakers crackled again. Koval’s voice, sharper this time. “Evacuate. Now.”

Ren hesitated. “Lila—”

She waved him off. “Go. I’ll handle this.”

Ren shot Elias a look, then bolted for the exit.

The lab door hissed shut behind him.

Lila turned to Elias. “You staged the rupture.”

Elias pulled a datapad from his pocket. The screen showed a schematic of the station’s fuel lines, red lines pulsing like veins. “Thermal imaging flagged the rupture five minutes ago. I just rerouted the heat signature to the pipe.”

Lila’s fingers curled into fists. “You wanted the lab empty.”

Elias tapped the screen. “You’ve got Julian’s team waiting on the Dry Glove Vault’s purge. The only way to get you alone in there is to clear the lab.”

Lila’s breath fogged in the cold air. “You’re not getting back in.”

Elias slid the datapad into his pocket. “I already am.”

The door to the lab’s observation deck slid open. A station crew member in a red parka stepped inside, his face pale.

“Dr. Cho,” he said. “Command wants you in the Core immediately.”

Lila didn’t look at him. “I’m aware.”

The crew member hesitated. “They want Elias Mercer too.”

Elias’s hand froze on his datapad. “Mercer?”

The crew member nodded. “Real name. Not Voss.”

Lila’s eyes flicked to Elias. “Get to the Core. Both of you.”

The crew member left, the door hissing shut behind him.

Elias exhaled. “They know.”

Lila’s voice was a blade. “They know something.”

Elias stepped toward her. “Julian’s team’s on site. They’re watching the vault. If they see you talking to me—”

“They’ll report it,” Lila finished. “Which means you’re already compromised.”

Elias didn’t answer. He turned to the observation deck, staring at the storm outside. The ice sheet shimmered under the station’s floodlights, undulating like a living thing.

Lila’s voice dropped. “You have two minutes to explain.”

Elias’s fingers flexed at his sides. “Two minutes?”

“Before Koval drags us both to the Core.”

Elias turned back to her. “Then you don’t have long.”

The speakers crackled again. Koval’s voice, urgent. “Dr. Cho. Elias Mercer. Status update.”

Lila’s jaw tightened. She pulled her radio from her belt, tapped the transmit button. “Cho and Mercer en route.”

She didn’t wait for confirmation. She strode to the door, her boots clicking on the metal floor.

Elias followed, his mind racing. The ice outside was too still. Too quiet.

Then he saw it.

A figure in a white parka, standing thirty yards from the station’s perimeter fence. Motionless. Watching.

Elias’s pulse spiked. “Lila.”

She didn’t stop. “What?”

“There’s someone out there.”

Lila turned, following his gaze. The figure didn’t move. Didn’t wave. Didn’t run.

Just stood there.

Lila’s radio crackled. Koval’s voice, sharp. “Cho. Mercer. Move.”

Lila tapped the transmit button. “Copy.”

She turned back to Elias, her face unreadable. “We’re out of time.”

The figure outside raised a hand.

Then the station’s floodlights died.

The Ice Lab plunged into darkness.

Chapter 8

The Dry Glove Vault’s heavy door groaned open to the sound of high heels on metal grating. Veronika Sloane stepped inside without waiting for an invitation, her briefcase swinging like a metronome against her thigh. The vault’s containment pods hummed, columns of blue light pulsing behind frosted glass.

Captain Renn Koval stood beside the nearest pod, arms crossed over her reinforced jacket. She didn’t offer a handshake. “You’re early.”

Veronika didn’t slow. “I’m on Mercer Trust time. That’s always early.”

Koval’s eyes flicked to the briefcase. “You went straight to Julian’s office before you boarded the flight.”

“CCO protocol.” Veronika set the case on the nearest console. “Irregularities in the last quarterly spill reports.”

Koval’s jaw tightened. “We’ve had no spills.”

Veronika thumbed the release on her case. The locks snapped back with a quiet click. Inside lay a tablet, a stylus, and a single sheet of paper folded into thirds. She tapped the screen. A live feed from the Geneva CCO server flickered to life.

Koval didn’t look at it. “This isn’t open for audit.”

“That’s not your call.” Veronika slid the paper across the console. “Subpoena. Elias Mercer. Real name. Not Voss.”

Koval didn’t touch it. “He’s a technician.”

Veronika’s fingers hovered over the tablet. “Former marine geologist. Disgraced after a field accident. Three DUIs in five years. No family. No assets.” She turned the screen so Koval could see the file header: Mercer. Elias Voss. Born Mercer. Son of Arthur Mercer. Legal heir to the Mercer Trust. Presumed dead in a mountaineering accident six years ago.

Koval’s gaze did not waver from the CCO feed. “He hasn’t been on station long enough to qualify for a subpoena.”

Veronika tapped the screen. A timestamp glowed red: 07:14 UTC. “Signed by Julian Mercer. Hand-delivered to my desk at 06:42. That’s twenty minutes old. Julian’s jet landed in Christchurch at 05:57.”

Koval exhaled through her nose. “You’re supposed to notify station command before you serve a subpoena on station soil.”

Veronika closed the case. “I did. Julian told me to bring it straight here.”

The pod behind Koval emitted a low hiss. A panel on its side slid open, revealing a column of frozen air. Inside rested a core sample, labeled in Julian’s handwriting: ROSS ICE SHELF – BLAKE TRENCH. DATE: 03/11/23.

Veronika didn’t react. “That’s not on your manifest.”

Koval didn’t move. “It’s a classified extraction.”

Veronika plucked the tablet from the case. “Classified under whose authority?”

Koval’s radio crackled. She didn’t answer. The static hissed again, louder this time. A voice cut through: “Captain. Fuel gauge discrepancy on the Vostok Barge. UN observers noticed a reroute to Sector 7.”

Koval’s thumb hovered over the transmit button. “How much?”

“Forty-two tons. Outside approved corridors.”

Veronika’s voice dropped. “Julian’s rerouting fuel to the barge before treaty ratification.”

Koval finally looked at her. “That’s proprietary data.”

Veronika tapped the tablet. “I have digital copies of every manifest from the last six months. Every barrel. Every corridor. Every signature.” She turned the screen toward Koval. “Including the one Julian signed to reroute forty-two tons to a non-approved sector.”

Koval’s fingers twitched toward her sidearm. She stopped halfway.

Veronika leaned in. “I was in the CCO when those manifests were altered. I know who approved them.”

Koval’s radio crackled again. The voice was different this time. Lila Cho. “Captain, we have a code red in the Ice Lab. Fuel leak. Emergency evacuation.”

Koval didn’t answer. She stared at Veronika.

Veronika didn’t blink. “Where’s Elias Mercer?”

Koval’s hand twitched. “Why?”

“Because Julian’s subpoena is for Elias Mercer. Not Elias Voss.”

Koval’s jaw locked. “He’s in the mess hall.”

Veronika’s briefcase snapped shut. “Take me to him.”

---

The mess hall smelled like burnt coffee and engine grease. A screen above the serving line displayed a distorted image of the Ice Lab’s rupture—a jagged line of steam shooting from a pipe like a geyser. Most of the crew had already evacuated. A few stragglers huddled at the far booth, whispering over cups of tea.

Elias stood at the coffee dispenser, his back to the room. His hands gripped the counter, knuckles white. He didn’t turn when the door hissed open.

Veronika Sloane stepped inside, briefcase swinging. Koval followed, boots echoing on the metal floor.

Elias poured coffee. The steam curled into the cold air. “Took you long enough.”

Veronika set the briefcase on a table. “Elias Mercer.”

He didn’t look up. “Voss is fine.”

“Your real name is Elias Mercer. Heir to the Mercer Trust.” She tapped the case. “Legal heir.”

Elias capped the coffee, turned. His face was blank. “I don’t know what you’re talking about.”

Koval crossed her arms. “Julian signed a subpoena for Elias Mercer this morning. You fit the description.”

Elias sipped the coffee. It scalded his tongue. He didn’t flinch. “Convenient.”

Veronika opened the case. The subpoena lay on top. “This is a legal document. Signed by Julian Mercer. Dated today.”

Elias set the cup down. “Julian’s been dead six years.”

Koval’s radio crackled. Lila’s voice, strained. “Captain, we’ve got a situation in the Core. Fuel depot failure. Rendezvous Point Delta’s compromised.”

Koval didn’t answer. She watched Elias.

Veronika slid the subpoena across the table. “Sign this. Acknowledge service. Then we’ll talk.”

Elias didn’t touch it. “Talk about what?”

Veronika pulled a datapad from her coat. The screen showed a file header: Mercer Trust Holdings – Quarterly Extraction Reports. Rows of numbers glowed red. “I have every manifest, every transfer, every signature. Including ones that don’t match station records.”

Elias wiped his hands on his coveralls, leaving dark streaks of grease. “You’re an auditor. You audit.”

Veronika tapped the screen. “I audit the numbers. I don’t audit the men who sign them.” She turned the pad so Elias could see a line item: TO: JULIAN MERCER. FROM: UNKNOWN. AMOUNT: 12,000,000 USD. DATE: 08/22/22. DESCRIPTION: CONSULTING FEES.

Elias’s fingers curled into fists. “That’s not my problem.”

Koval’s voice cut in. “Elias.”

He turned. Koval stood between him and the door, her sidearm half-drawn.

Veronika didn’t move. “He knows.”

Koval’s radio crackled again. Lila’s voice, urgent. “Captain, the depot’s breached. We’re losing pressure. All hands to shelter.”

The lights flickered. The mess hall’s emergency lights sparked to life, casting long shadows across the floor.

Elias looked at Veronika. Then at Koval. Then back at Veronika.

“You’re not here to audit,” he said. “You’re here to bury me before I bury Julian.”

Veronika didn’t answer.

Koval’s sidearm clicked into full draw. “Elias Mercer, you’re in violation of Mercer Trust corporate policy. You will accompany us to the Core for questioning.”

Elias didn’t move. “I already have the throne.”

The lights went out.

A gunshot echoed through the mess hall. The sound was swallowed by the storm outside, but the muzzle flash lit the room for half a second—a white bloom against the darkness.

When the lights returned, Veronika’s briefcase lay on the floor, the lock broken. The subpoena was gone.

And Elias was gone.

Chapter 9

The mess hall’s emergency lights cast a sickly yellow glow over the abandoned tables. Steam from the ruptured fuel line still curled against the ceiling, mixing with the greasy scent of burnt popcorn left in the dispenser. Elias pushed through the swinging door into the kitchenette, boots squeaking on the vinyl floor. A single fluorescent tube flickered above the fryer, buzzing like a dying insect.

Lila Cho leaned against the stainless-steel counter, arms crossed, her parka zipped to the throat despite the inadequate heating. She didn’t move when he entered.

Elias grabbed a chipped mug from the rack and filled it from the tap. Water ran brown before clearing. He drank anyway.

Lila’s voice cut through the silence. “You’re not going to drink yourself to death down here. Not after six years of pretending.”

He set the mug down, harder than necessary. “What do you want, Lila?”

She uncrossed her arms. “The same thing you do. To finish what we started.”

Elias turned. The fridge hummed between them. “We started nothing.”

“You filed three research proposals last month. All denied. All related to Blake Trench.” She tapped her wrist terminal. A screen flickered to life, displaying a title: *Director of Geophysics – McMurdo Station.* Below it, a salary figure large enough to make his breath catch. “Julian’s offer.”

Elias didn’t look at the screen. “I’m not interested.”

Lila tucked the terminal away. “You’re in no position to refuse. Koval’s report to Geneva called your behavior ‘erratic and destabilizing.’ The CCO has flagged your access.”

Elias wiped his hands on his thighs. Grease smeared the fabric. “Then revoke it. I don’t need Koval’s permission to exist.”

“You need paper to breathe down here.” Lila stepped closer, voice dropping. “Julian wants you back in the fold. Properly. With oversight. No more rogue data drops. No more unapproved samples.” She tilted her head toward the camera mounted in the corner. “There’s a reason you’re still breathing, Elias. It’s not because you’re clever. It’s because he’s playing a longer game.”

Elias picked up the mug again. The water had gone cold. He set it down. “Julian’s not alive.”

Lila’s smile didn’t reach her eyes. “He’s alive enough to sign your contract. Alive enough to sign your death warrant if you cross him again.” She pulled a slim envelope from her pocket and slid it across the counter. “One signature. Return to the trust as Director. Full clearance. Full credit. Full pension. Or—” She tapped the envelope. “—you walk out of here with a one-way ticket to Vostok Station. No recourse. No appeal. No data.”

Elias stared at the envelope. It was thinner than it should be. No employment contract. Just a ticket and a set of station orders stamped in red.

He picked it up. The paper crinkled in his fingers. “And if I say no?”

Lila’s gaze didn’t waver. “Then you’ll be found in the ice trench tomorrow morning. Officially, a suicide. Unofficially, a mercy killing for a man who’s been dead six years.”

Elias dropped the envelope. It fluttered to the floor between them like a dead leaf.

Lila didn’t flinch. “I didn’t come here to debate.”

He crouched slowly, picked up the envelope, and tore it in half. The sound was sharp in the small room.

Lila’s jaw tightened. “That’s your last chance.”

Elias stood. “I already have the throne.”

She stared at him. A beat of silence. Then she laughed—a short, sharp sound. “You think this is a threat? A bluff?” She tapped her terminal again. The screen changed, displaying a feed from the Geneva stock exchange: *Mercer Trust Holdings surged 3.2% on news of Julian Mercer’s return to operational control.*

Elias didn’t react.

Lila leaned in. “Julian’s back. Real back. Not a ghost. Not a fraud. The real thing. And he’s signed your revocation order.” She toggled the feed. A document appeared: *Termination of Employment – Elias Mercer (aka Elias Voss) – Effective immediately.*

Elias’s fingers twitched.

Lila tapped the screen. “You have twelve hours to sign the Director’s contract. After that, your access codes are flushed. Your quarters are sealed. Your samples are purged. And you become irrelevant.”

Elias looked at the torn envelope in his hand. The ticket fluttered in the draft from the kitchen door.

Lila’s voice softened. “You were always going to lose, Elias. You just didn’t know it yet. This is the only way you walk out of here alive.”

He stepped closer. Close enough to smell her perfume—cheap, industrial. Close enough to see the faint scar above her left eyebrow, a souvenir from a field accident years ago, one she never talked about.

“Then I’ll die here,” he said.

Lila’s fingers hovered over her terminal. “You don’t have the guts.”

Elias leaned down until their foreheads almost touched. His voice was a rasp. “You don’t know what I’ve done.”

She didn’t pull back. “Try me.”

A shadow passed over the doorway. Lila’s eyes flicked past him.

Elias didn’t turn. He didn’t need to.

Veronika Sloane stood in the doorway, briefcase in hand, eyes locked on Lila. “The captain wants to see you both. Now.”

Lila didn’t react. “Fine. But Elias is coming with us.”

Veronika didn’t move. “He’s not invited.”

Lila’s hand twitched toward her sidearm. “He’s under corporate hold.”

Veronika didn’t blink. “Not anymore.”

Elias exhaled. The sound was almost a laugh.

Lila’s hand settled on her holster. “You’re bluffing.”

Veronika tapped her wrist terminal. A single word appeared on the screen: *Released.*

Lila’s fingers froze on the grip of her pistol.

Veronika looked at Elias. “Grab your coat.”

Lila’s voice cracked. “You can’t release him. Julian signed the revocation.”

Veronika didn’t look at her. “Julian signed your revocation too, Lila. Effective immediately. For obstruction and falsification of environmental impact reports.”

Lila paled. Her hand fell away from the pistol. “That’s not possible.”

Veronika tapped her terminal again. A live feed from Geneva flickered to life on the kitchen screen: *BREAKING – Mercer Trust accused of falsifying UN environmental compliance documents. Documents match filed reports by marine geologist Elias Mercer.*

Lila’s breath hitched. She didn’t look at the screen.

Veronika ticked off points on her fingers. “Sample ROSS-ICE-BLAKE-TRENCH-031123. Fuel reroute to Sector 7. Quarterly spill reports falsified. Mineral rights patents filed under Julian Mercer’s name while Elias authored the data.” She looked at Elias. “You’ve been busy.”

Lila’s voice was a whisper. “You can’t prove any of that.”

Veronika tapped her terminal. A green checkmark appeared beside each bullet point. “Already did. CCO has the full chain of custody. Geneva court has subpoenaed every record. UN observers are boarding the Vostok Barge as we speak.”

Lila’s knees buckled. She grabbed the counter to steady herself.

Elias picked up his coat from the back of a chair. He put it on slowly, like a man dressing for his own funeral.

Veronika’s voice was quiet. “You have two choices, Lila. Cooperate with the CCO audit or face obstruction charges. Either way, you’re done.”

Lila didn’t answer. She stared at the screen, at the numbers scrolling past, at the name *Elias Mercer* attached to every violation.

Elias zipped his coat. The sound was final.

Veronika stepped aside. “After you.”

Lila didn’t move.

Elias walked past her, close enough to feel the draft of her panic. He paused at the doorway.

Lila’s voice followed him, trembling. “You’re making a mistake.”

Elias looked back. “I already have the throne.”

He walked out. The door hissed shut behind him.

Veronika followed.

Lila stood alone in the flickering light of the kitchenette, the sound of footsteps receding down the hall. She reached into her pocket and pulled out her sidearm. She didn’t point it. She didn’t need to.

The screen above her flickered again. A new alert: *Mercer Trust Holdings shares plummet 12% on environmental fraud allegations.*

Lila’s finger hovered over the trigger.

Then the lights went out.

Chapter 10

The library smelled of ozone and old paper. Elias typed in the dark, only the glow of his terminal illuminating his face. The station’s Wi-Fi was locked down after the Geneva leak, but the hardline in the corner still hummed with life. He’d jury-rigged a Faraday cage from copper wiring and a galvanized bucket. Ugly, but it worked.

His fingers moved fast. A secure shell into a dead drop in Geneva. Another into a proxy server in Santiago. A third, buried deep, pulsed a signal toward a smuggler’s vessel lurking in the Ross Sea. 72 hours. That’s all it would take.

A shadow moved in the corridor outside. The light strip flickered. Elias didn’t look up.

“You’re late.”

Daniel Reeves’ voice crackled through the earpiece. Static. Then clearer.

Elias cracked his knuckles. “Traffic.”

“Traffic in McMurdo? You’re the only traffic here.” Daniel’s laugh was a dry rasp. “You really think Julian won’t notice the spike in external comms?”

Elias minimized the window. Opened the UN feed. A courtroom in Geneva. A judge’s gavel. A lawyer for Mercer Trust denying everything.

“He’s already noticed,” Elias said. “That’s the point.”

Daniel exhaled into the mic. “You’re playing with fire. If they trace this—”

“They won’t.” Elias pulled up the encrypted ledger. A list of names. Journalists. Lawyers. An editor at *The Guardian* who owed him a favor. “I’ve got six people with burner phones. Six people who don’t know each other. One fire, six matches.”

“And if one match doesn’t strike?”

Elias closed the ledger. “Then I burn alone.”

A pause. The kind where Daniel was either weighing the odds or checking for surveillance. Elias didn’t care which.

“You really think you can walk away from this?” Daniel asked. “After six years? After Julian?”

Elias minimized the window. Opened a new one. A live feed from the station’s security hub. Lila’s terminal, logged in as admin. A progress bar: *Dry Glove Vault purge – 98% complete.*

“It’s already done,” Elias said.

Another shadow in the corridor. Closer this time. Footsteps. Slow. Deliberate.

“You’re broadcasting from the library terminal.” Daniel’s voice dropped. “That’s a risk.”

“Not if you know where to look.” Elias swiveled the monitor slightly. The Faraday cage wasn’t perfect. A few stray packets leaked. But it wasn’t enough to triangulate. Not yet.

The footsteps stopped outside the door. A knock. Three raps. Pause. Two raps.

Elias minimized the UN feed. The courtroom vanished. He pulled up a blank screen. Typed a command. The monitor went black.

“Come in,” he said.

The door slid open. A gust of cold air rushed in. Captain Renn Koval stepped inside, his parka dusted with ice. He didn’t close the door behind him.

“You’re not supposed to be in here,” Koval said.

Elias didn’t turn around. “Neither are you.”

Koval shut the door. The lock clicked. “Lila’s gone to ground. Veronika’s under house arrest in Med Bay. I’m officially relieved of command.”

Elias minimized the terminal. Turned in his chair. “Congratulations. You win.”

Koval’s gaze flicked to the monitor. Black screen. No activity. “You’re the only one left with admin access.”

Elias shrugged. “Not for long.”

Koval stepped closer. The floor creaked under his boots. “You really think you can take Mercer Trust from a research station?”

Elias leaned back. The chair squeaked. “I already did.”

Koval’s jaw tightened. “Julian’s barge is en route. Fuel rerouted. He’s coming to clean house.”

“He’s coming to die.”

Koval’s hand twitched toward his sidearm. “You don’t have the data anymore. The purge ran.”

Elias tapped his wrist terminal. A file appeared on the screen. *ROSS-ICE-BLAKE-TRENCH-031123.*

Koval’s face went slack. “That’s impossible.”

Elias minimized the file. “Possible enough.”

Koval’s eyes flicked to the terminal again. Then to Elias. “You’re broadcasting.”

Elias didn’t deny it. “Not for long.”

Koval pulled his sidearm. “Step away from the terminal.”

Elias didn’t move. “You won’t shoot me.”

“Try me.”

Elias looked at the gun. Then at Koval’s face. “You think I care if you do? You think I haven’t already sent what you need to Geneva? To Santiago? To every terminal on this continent?” He tapped his wrist terminal. “The UN already has the chain of custody. Julian’s orders. The fuel logs. The falsified reports. Hell, I even sent them Julian’s signature on my revocation order.”

Koval’s finger tightened on the trigger. “You’re bluffing.”

Elias opened the UN feed again. The courtroom reappeared. The lawyer for Mercer Trust was on his feet, arguing. The judge banged the gavel.

Koval’s voice was a rasp. “That’s not—”

“Live,” Elias said. “Julian’s watching it right now. In real time. He knows the jig is up.”

Koval’s hand shook. The gun wavered. “You’re not him. You can’t be.”

Elias stood. The chair rolled back. “I’m not Julian. But I’m not Elias Voss either.”

Koval’s finger twitched. “Then who the hell are you?”

Elias zipped his parka. “The heir.”

The terminal screen flickered. A new alert appeared in red:

*SECURITY BREACH: TERMINAL 7 – LIBRARY.*

Koval’s eyes flicked to the screen. Then to Elias.

The door behind Koval slid open.

Lila Cho stood in the doorway, her sidearm leveled at Koval’s back. Her other hand held a terminal. A progress bar pulsed on the screen: *Dry Glove Vault purge – 100%.*

Koval didn’t turn around. “Lila—”

She didn’t lower the gun. “Step away from him.”

Koval’s finger tightened on the trigger. “You’re making a mistake.”

Lila’s voice was flat. “No. But you are.”

The terminal screen behind Elias flashed again. A new alert:

*UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS: E. MERCER (ADMIN OVERRIDE).*

Elias didn’t move. Didn’t react. The screen glowed in the dark, casting long shadows.

Koval’s breath hitched. “What the hell is that?”

Lila’s eyes flicked to the screen. Her face went pale. The gun in her hand didn’t waver, but her finger trembled on the trigger.

Elias smiled. Just a little. “You’re out of time.”

The lights flickered. Then died.

Chapter 11

The corridors of McMurdo Station were too quiet. No alarms. No shouting. Just the low hum of the ventilation system and the occasional groan of metal expanding in the cold. Elias walked fast, his breath fogging the faceplate of his hood. The Dry Glove Vault was two levels down, past the Ice Lab. Security should have been locking down after the purge. But the purge had failed.

A door hissed open behind him. Boots on metal grates. He didn’t turn.

“Voss.”

No answer.

“Elias.” Lila’s voice cut through the static of his hood’s comm. “You’re not supposed to be—”

He shouldered through the lab doors into the Ice Lab. The room was dark, only emergency lights bleeding through the window slits. Displays flickered—some alive, some dead. The purge had taken half the systems offline. The other half were behaving erratically.

Lila stepped in after him, her parka splattered with melted ice. She held a terminal in one hand, her sidearm in the other. “You really think you can walk out of here with what’s left?”

Elias didn’t slow. “I’m not walking out. I’m moving forward.”

She blocked his path to the Vault door. “The purge completed. All the Mercer data is gone.”

“No.” Elias tapped the wrist terminal clipped to his sleeve. A readout scrolled: *Dry Glove Vault: 87% integrity.* “You didn’t delete it. You only reset the access logs.”

Her face tightened. “You don’t know what you’re looking at.”

“Then show me.”

She raised the gun. “I will shoot you.”

The muzzle didn’t waver. Neither did his glare. “You won’t.”

A door at the far end of the lab slid open. Veronika Sloane stepped in, her jumpsuit unzipped to the waist, breath ragged. She held a data slate in one hand, a knife in the other.

Lila didn’t lower the gun. “You get paid to cause problems, Verna, or are you just here for the circus?”

Veronika didn’t flinch. “Koval’s under arrest. Mercer barge is forty klicks out. Fuel reroute reversed. Julian’s going to be a very unhappy man when he finds out his pet captain got played.”

Elias kept moving. Veronika stepped aside, eyes on Lila.

“He’s bluffing,” Lila said. “He doesn’t have the data. He can’t.”

Elias reached the Vault door. The biometric scanner lit up red. He pressed his glove to the plate. Green.

Lila’s voice cracked. “No. That’s impossible.”

The door hissed open.

Inside, the Vault was a tomb of servers and black racks. Emergency lights pulsed like a dying heartbeat. The purge had killed the main array, but the backup cores were still breathing. Red LEDs blinked in the dark.

Elias pulled the encrypted drive from his pocket. Slid it into the primary slot.

The system beeped. A terminal lit up across the room.

Lila stepped inside, the gun still up. “You think plugging a drive into a dead system will bring it back?”

“No.” He tapped commands into his wrist terminal. “I think giving it a reason to wake up will.”

The terminal beeped again. Then again. A progress bar appeared on the main screen:

*PURGE REVERSAL INITIATING (17%).*

Lila’s voice went thin. “You can’t reverse a purge.”

Veronika leaned against the doorframe. “Looks like he just did.”

Lila’s finger twitched on the trigger. “Step away from the terminal.”

Elias didn’t move. The progress bar climbed: 32%. 47%. 61%.

“You’re making a mistake,” Lila said.

Veronika laughed. Short. Sharp. “Lady, you’ve been making mistakes since day one. This is just the one that matters.”

The progress bar hit 89%.

Lila’s jaw clenched. “You don’t have Julian’s admission. You don’t have the fuel logs. You don’t have the revocation order.”

Elias didn’t look up. “Check the Geneva feed.”

The terminal screen flashed. A new window popped open. A courtroom. A judge. A lawyer for Mercer Trust on his feet, arguing.

Lila’s breath hitched. “That’s not—”

“Live,” Elias said. “Julian’s watching it right now. He knows.”

The progress bar hit 97%.

Lila’s gun arm shook. “You’re broadcasting from a dead system. No signal strength. No redundancy. They’ll triangulate this in minutes.”

Elias tapped the wrist terminal. A new feed appeared in the corner of the main screen. A smuggler’s vessel, rolling in the Ross Sea. A single line of text beneath it:

*DATA TRANSFER INITIATING. 12 TERMINALS. 6 COUNTRIES. 0 TRACE.*

Lila’s face went white. “No.”

Veronika whistled. “Damn.”

The progress bar hit 100%.

The main terminal screen flashed red.

*AUTHENTICATION FAILED: E. MERCER (ADMIN OVERRIDE).*

The lights died.

A gunshot cracked in the dark.

Elias felt the air move past his ear. Heard the bullet ricochet off the ceiling. Then Lila’s voice, raw and furious.

“You’re dead.”

Chapter 12

The Ice Lab terminal glowed in the dark, its screen casting sharp blue light across Elias’s face. The Geneva feed flickered—pixelated, lagging, but clear enough. A courtroom. A panel of judges in Geneva. A Mercer lawyer standing, hands folded, calm. Julian wasn’t visible. Not yet.

“You think this is enough?” Lila’s voice was a blade, low and steady. She hadn’t holstered the gun. “A grainy feed from a dead station? No chain of custody. No verification. They’ll dismiss it as a hack.”

Elias tapped the comm key on his wrist terminal. “Then why did Julian just lock down all Mercer Trust comms?”

Veronika leaned against the server rack, arms crossed. “Because he knows what’s coming.”

The feed pixelated again. Then stabilized. A new window opened—a second screen, split from the first. This one showed Julian Mercer, live, in a private office somewhere, mic muted, eyes locked on a terminal. His face was pale, lips pressed tight.

Elias keyed his comm. “Julian. You see this?”

Julian didn’t answer. His fingers flew across his keyboard. The Geneva feed stuttered. The Mercer lawyer faltered mid-sentence.

Veronika exhaled through her nose. “He’s in full damage control. Cutting feeds. Spinning the narrative.”

Lila shifted her weight, gun still raised. “You’re broadcasting from the Ice Lab. Your signal is traceable. Koval will have your head before the Geneva panel finishes adjourning.”

Elias ignored her. He opened the encrypted file. The screen filled with Julian’s signature. The forged avalanche report. The doctored UN compliance filings. The bank transfer logs. Every piece Elias had buried for five years.

Lila’s voice dropped. “You don’t have the originals. This is all digital. Circumstantial.”

Elias selected the Geneva feed window. Scrolled the courtroom transcript. “Page 12. Exhibit B: Mercer Trust internal memo. Dated three years ago. Julian’s handwriting. Calls for ‘eliminating the liability in the field.’ That’s you, Julian. That’s me. That’s anyone who stands in your way.”

Julian’s screen flickered again. He leaned toward his camera, eyes wide. His voice crackled through the comm, raw: “You—you don’t understand what you’re doing.”

Elias smiled. “Then explain it. Right now. To the panel. To me.”

Julian’s jaw clenched. He reached off-camera. The feed stabilized. The Mercer lawyer stood, cleared his throat. “Your Honor, we have reason to believe this feed has been compromised—”

“Shut up,” Julian said into his mic, voice trembling. “I’ll handle this.”

Elias zoomed the camera in. The Geneva panel leaned forward, murmuring. The judge banged a gavel. “Mr. Mercer. You are on record. You may proceed.”

Julian’s breath came fast. He looked directly at the camera—at Elias. “You’re not him. You’re a ghost. A—”

Elias selected another file. Julian’s private email. The one sending the barge to the ice shelf. The one rerouting fuel through Koval’s channels. The one ordering the purge of the Dry Glove Vault.

He transmitted.

The screen refreshed. A new window opened. A status bar: *File transmitted. Geneva Court: Evidence accepted. Case opened.*

Julian’s face went slack. His hands flew to his keyboard. The feed stuttered. The Mercer lawyer started speaking, but Julian cut him off. “No. No no no—”

Elias watched as Julian’s terminal icons flashed red. His screen split into six smaller windows. Each one showed a different jurisdiction: Geneva. The Hague. New York. London. Sydney. A sixth, blocked out, labeled *REDACTED*.

Julian’s breath hitched. “You think you’ve won?”

Elias didn’t answer. He pulled the encrypted flash drive from his pocket. Slid it into the terminal. The system beeped. A window opened.

*MERCER TRUST DISSOLUTION ORDER: PENDING.*

Julian’s screen flashed. A new alert appeared across all six windows.

*TRUST ASSETS SEIZED. CONTROL TRANSFERRED TO LEGAL HEIR: ELIAS V. MERCER.*

Julian’s voice cracked. “This isn’t—this is impossible.”

Elias watched as Julian’s face drained of color. The Geneva feed pixelated again. The Mercer lawyer stumbled. The judge called for a recess.

Veronika let out a low whistle. “Damn. He’s melting.”

Lila’s gun arm didn’t waver, but her voice did. “You just triggered a trust dissolution. That’s—”

“Irreversible,” Elias said.

Julian’s screen went dark. For a full breath, nothing. Then, a single line of text, flashing red, appearing across every window:

*ASSETS CONFISCATED. CEO DETAINED. CASE FILE TRANSFERRED.*

Julian’s voice was a whisper, broken. “You don’t know what you’ve done.”

Elias watched as Julian’s terminal powered down. The Geneva feed cut to static. The courtroom dissolved into pixelated noise. The screen went dark.

Then, a new message, from an unknown terminal, in bold white letters:

EVIDENCE FILE: MERCER VS. MERCER—CASE OPENED.

Lila’s breath hitched. Veronika stepped forward, eyes on the screen. “That’s it. That’s the kill shot.”

Elias didn’t move. He watched Julian’s terminal glow in the dark—once, twice—before the screen went black.

A new alarm blared from the comms. Not from the Geneva feed. From the station. A shrill, pulsing tone Elias hadn’t heard in years.

*SECURITY BREACH: GENEVA COMMAND OVERRIDE.*

Lila’s eyes flicked to the terminal. To Elias. To the door. “That’s not internal. That’s—”

A voice cut through the comms. Not Julian’s. Not Geneva’s.

Captain Renn Koval, sharp and clipped: “All personnel. Report to Black Ridge immediately. Evacuation protocols initiated. This is not a drill.”

Elias turned to the terminal. The screen flickered. A new window opened. A satellite ping.

*DRIVE #2 DECRYPTED. FILES TRANSMITTED.*

The terminal beeped once. Then went dark.

The lights in the Ice Lab flickered. The emergency systems groaned. The comms erupted in overlapping voices—orders, curses, panic.

Lila lowered the gun an inch. “What the hell did you just do?”

Elias looked at the blank screen. Then at Lila. Then at the door.

Outside, the station alarms wailed. The sound of boots on metal grates grew louder. Closer.

Veronika grabbed his arm. “Elias. We’ve got—”

A new sound cut her off. Not an alarm. Not a voice. A metal door at the far end of the Ice Lab hissed open. Cold air rushed in. The emergency lights flickered again.

Then the lights went out.

The last thing Elias saw before the terminal died was the warning message, flashing once, in the dark:

*STATION CLEAR.*

Chapter 13

Black Ridge Dormitory – Lower Level – Emergency Lighting

The corridor smelled like ozone and sweat. Emergency exit signs cast red stripes across Lila’s face as she leaned against the wall, cigarette smoke curling from her fingers despite the fire suppression alarms. She took a long drag, exhaled through her nose.

“You realize you just burned the station down around our ears,” she said.

Koval stood at the far end of the hallway, helmet in hand, boots planted apart. Her mouthpiece glinted under the red light. “Not my station anymore.”

Lila flicked ash onto the floor. “You could’ve stopped this.”

“With what? A phone call to Geneva?” Koval’s laugh was sharp. “They already know. So does every comms terminal on the continent.”

A shout echoed down the hall—someone running, then the thud of a body hitting the bulkhead. Lila didn’t flinch.

“Julian’s in custody,” Koval said. “Geneva’s already got him. Mercer Trust is frozen. Your badge is worthless.”

Lila crushed the cigarette under her boot. “And my research?”

Koval’s eyes flicked toward the emergency stairwell. “Gone. Sealed in the Ice Lab.”

Lila pushed off the wall. “You let them?”

“I sealed it myself.” Koval adjusted her glove. “Protocol. Evidence chain. You know how this works.”

Lila’s fingers twitched. “You’re throwing the game because you lost.”

“Game’s over.” Koval tapped her wrist display. “Evacuation underway. Fuel’s gone. Power’s failing. Two hours till McMurdo’s a block of ice.”

A new alarm blared—different tone, sharper. The station’s primary systems were shutting down in sequence.

Lila pulled a small black device from her pocket. A data burner. “I could walk out with what’s left.”

Koval didn’t move. “To where? The skidoos are disabled. The helo’s grounded. The ice shelf is a kill box.”

Lila thumbed the device. “You’re enjoying this.”

Koval’s jaw tightened. “I’m doing my job.”

“Your job was to keep this place running.”

“My job was to follow orders.” Koval gestured down the hall. “Julian’s orders. Now his orders don’t exist.”

Lila stared at the burner. The screen flickered—dead. She pocketed it.

“You burned your badge,” Koval said. “That’s not protocol.”

Lila didn’t answer. She pulled the Mercer Trust insignia from her collar and dropped it onto the floor. It clinked against the grating.

Koval exhaled through her nose. “You’re making a mistake.”

“No,” Lila said. “I’m making sure there’s nothing left to mistake.”

A distant metal groan shuddered through the corridor. The station’s backbone was folding.

Koval’s wrist terminal buzzed. She read the screen, then looked up. “Second wave coming. Ventilation shafts are sealing.”

Lila stepped toward the stairwell. “Last chance to walk with me.”

Koval didn’t move. “I’m not running.”

Lila opened the stairwell door. The cold air hit her face like a slap. She paused.

“Then you’re staying.” She stepped through. The door swung shut behind her.

The emergency lights dimmed further. The station groaned again—deep, structural. Metal protesting.

Koval watched the door close. Her hand went to her sidearm, then away. She pulled her comm unit.

“Ice Lab, this is Koval. Status check.”

Static. Then a voice—Elias’s.

“Station’s dead. Just like you wanted.”

Koval’s grip tightened. “Mercer.”

A pause. Then Elias again, softer. “Captain.”

“You don’t know what you’ve done.”

The comm crackled. A new sound—data, streaming. Fast. Too fast for voice.

Koval’s wrist terminal lit up. A satellite ping.

*DRIVE #2 DECRYPTED. FILES TRANSMITTED. STATION CLEAR.*

The screen went dark. The emergency lights died.

The last thing Koval saw was the door to the stairwell, red-lit, already swinging open from the other side. A gust of wind—real wind, not ventilation—rushed in.

Then the lights cut out completely.

The station’s last breath was a whisper of metal settling. The kind of sound that lingers in the dark.

Chapter 14

McMurdo Station Core – Command Hub – Six Weeks After Evacuation

The command hub smelled like antiseptic and old coffee. The emergency lights had been replaced with standard fluorescents, but the hum of failing generators still rattled through the walls. Elias sat in Koval’s chair, boots on the desk, fingers tapping the armrest. The Mercer Trust insignia had been stripped from the door frame. The only thing left was a faded stencil: *Station Core – Do Not Enter*.

Lila stood at the far end of the room, arms crossed. She wore a borrowed parka, hood down. Her badge was gone. The data burner in her pocket was empty.

“You could’ve let me walk,” she said.

Elias didn’t look up. “I did.”

“You burned the station to the ground.”

“Protocol.” He tapped the desk. “Evidence chain. You know how this works.”

Her fingers twitched. “You didn’t have to seal the Ice Lab.”

“Yes, I did.” He finally met her eyes. “Your research is gone. Your work, your reputation, the last ten years of Mercer Trust’s lies. All of it.”

She exhaled through her nose. “And the assays? The mineral reports?”

“Already on a plane to Geneva.” He tapped the second drive clipped to his belt. “Encrypted. Time-stamped. Julian’s signature on every falsified compliance sheet.”

Lila stepped closer. “You’re handing everything to the UN.”

“Not everything.” He set the drive on the desk between them. “Your amnesty.”

She didn’t touch it. “In exchange for what?”

“Full disclosure.” Elias leaned forward. “Every directive you followed. Every report you buried. Every lie you told to keep me from the field.”

Her jaw tightened. “You don’t need this.”

“I need you to say it.” He gestured to the drive. “On record. No edits. No omissions.”

A long silence. The generators hummed again, a wet, labored sound.

Lila unzipped her parka. The inside pocket of her shirt was bulging. She pulled out a second burner, smaller than the first, and tossed it onto the desk. It skidded toward him.

“Julian’s private comms,” she said. “Every encrypted log from the past three years. His orders to Koval. Your brother’s medical files. The real accident report.”

Elias picked it up. The casing was warm. “You kept this?”

“After you were declared dead, Julian wanted proof.” She rubbed her temple. “I gave him the wrong files. Kept the ones that mattered.”

He set the burner next to the other drive. “Why?”

“Because I knew you’d come back.” Her voice was flat. “And because I was tired of lying to myself.”

Elias stood. He walked to the window. The reinforced glass was frosted with condensation. Beyond it, the ice shelf stretched flat and endless under a pale Antarctic sun. No station. No people. Just wind carving patterns into the surface.

He turned back. “You’re not getting a promotion.”

“I don’t want one.”

“You’re not getting a pension.”

“Fine.”

“You’re not getting paid for this.”

She almost smiled. “I haven’t been paid in months.”

Elias picked up the drive with Julian’s comms. He walked to the station’s primary terminal. The screen flickered to life—still online, still connected to Mercer’s old satellites. He inserted the drive. A file structure loaded. Hundreds of entries. Hundreds of lies.

He looked at Lila. “You ready?”

She didn’t blink. “Do it.”

He pressed Enter.

The terminal beeped. A progress bar appeared. *Initializing decryption protocol.*

“It’s going to the UN,” he said. “All of it. Julian’s orders. Your compliance. The whole conspiracy.”

Lila watched the bar fill. “And then?”

“Then Mercer Trust is dead.” He ejected the drive and held it up. “No more mining. No more patents. No more Mercer.”

A shadow passed over her face. “What do you do with an empire?”

Elias dropped the drive into the recycling chute beneath the terminal. It vanished with a quiet *clunk*.

He turned to her. “Dismantle it.”

The terminal let out a long, electronic beep. The decryption complete. A single message appeared on screen:

**FILES TRANSMITTED. STATION CLEAR.**

Lila stared at the words. Her breath fogged the glass of the terminal.

Elias walked back to the desk. He picked up the other drive—the one with the mineral reports—and clipped it to his belt.

“You’re not staying,” she said.

“No.”

“You’re not Julian.”

“Never was.” He pulled on his gloves. “You should leave before the transport gets here.”

Lila didn’t move. “What happens to me?”

Elias paused at the door. “You go home. Or you find something new. Or you disappear.” He opened the door. Cold air rushed in. “But you don’t work for Mercer anymore.”

She looked down at the floor. At the scattered badges and papers still littering the hub. At the faint stain where Koval’s insignia had been burned away.

Then she looked up. “What about you?”

Elias stepped into the hall. The emergency lights flickered. Somewhere deep in the station, a pipe groaned under pressure.

He didn’t answer.

Chapter 15

The transport skidded to a stop on the ice outside McMurdo Station Core. Elias stepped into the knee-deep snow, his boots sinking before finding purchase. The door hissed shut behind him. The driver didn’t look back.

The station loomed ahead, a cluster of prefab buildings dwarfed by the ice shelf. No smoke from the stacks. No figures in orange parkas moving between domes. Just silence and the wind scraping at the corrugated metal.
